exemplar.title: Probability and Expected Value in British TV show 'Deal or No Deal' to predict Banker Behaviours

detailedExemplarData.title: Probability and Expected Value in British TV show 'Deal or No Deal' to predict Banker Behaviours

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear sequence with introduction, investigation, simulation and conclusion reinforcing logical flow
Wellâstructured with a contents page, labelled headings/subheadings and appropriately placed diagrams/tables
Generally coherent presentation that guides the reader through the bankerâbehaviour model
weaknesses: Occasional minor digressions and repeated formulae undermine conciseness
Formatting inconsistencies in tables and bibliography reduce polish
Some sections (e.g., Simulation Round 2) feel abruptly cut
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (coherence; organization) sit firmly at band 3, while conciseness is at band 2 due to unnecessary repetitions. Overall, the presentation is well organized and logically developed but falls short of the exceptional clarity and brevity required for a band 4.

strengths: Effective use of multiple representations (tables, scatter plots, regression line) linked to the narrative
Reasonable logical progression from data collection through regression modelling
Correct notation appears often enough to support understanding
weaknesses: Inconsistent mathematical notation (misplaced symbols, variable names)
Missing axis labels and abrupt transitions weaken clarity
Computational slips occasionally obscure logical flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Representations are effective (band 3) and some correct mathematical language is used, but inconsistency and clarity errors prevent a band 4. Banded at 3 to acknowledge strong multiâmodal communication combined with areas for refinement.

strengths: Designing and running custom simulations demonstrates genuine independent thinking
Personal context (trainer sessions) informs variable selection, showing a clear personal approach
Integration of psychological commentary evidences creativity and initiative
weaknesses: Justification for simulation sample size is not provided
Approach, while thoughtful, relies on fairly standard methods
Creativity is moderate rather than highly innovative
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands are at band 2: significant independent thinking, a clear personal approach and good creativity. The work is engaged and personal but not at the level of outstanding originality or initiative.

strengths: Reflection on fairness, volatility and psychological manipulation is present throughout
Thoughtful analysis of banker behaviour and EV implications
Clear evaluation of limitations and impact on conclusions
weaknesses: Critical reflection lacks depth and quantitative linkage to the simulation data
Future implications are only lightly touched upon
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand falls at band 2: meaningful reflection, thoughtful (but not deep) analysis and clear outcome evaluation. The reflections are solid but do not reach the substantial critical depth or comprehensive future planning required for higher bands.

strengths: Relevant probability, expected value and regression methods are consistently applied
Mathematics is fully commensurate with SL level and goes beyond trivial arithmetic
Good understanding with only minor computational or formatting errors
weaknesses: Several numerical errors and inconsistent denominators reveal minor understanding gaps
Key statistics (e.g., RÂ², pâvalues) are omitted in regression discussion
Occasional misuse of permutation formula instead of direct probability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands for relevance and level are at band 4 (appropriate and courseâlevel mathematics); understanding is at band 3 due to minor errors. Taken together, the use of mathematics aligns with band 4âeffective, appropriate and commensurate with SL expectations.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations usually follow a logical order (data â regression â bounds) but clarity is weakened by computational slips (incorrect EV total, factorial misuse) and abrupt transitions.  Hence only âsome logical structure and clarityâ are demonstrated.

The student usually applies formulae correctly (e.g., regression gradient), but several numerical errors (e.g., EV = $131 477 mis-copied list, mistaken factorial interpretation) reveal incomplete understanding.  Thus only âsome understanding with errorsâ is achieved.

The student employs tables, scatter plots, a regression line, and calculated bounds, each clearly linked to the narrative.  These representations aid understanding and are mostly appropriate, earning the âeffectiveâ descriptor.

The exploration follows a clear sequence (introduction, rules, investigation, simulation, conclusion) and each section reasonably prepares the reader for the next.  Although some calculations are clumsily laid out, the argument about banker behaviour develops logically from data collection to regression modelling.  Frequent minor digressions and occasional duplicated work (e.g., the very long EV summation) prevent it from reaching the âexcellentâ descriptor, yet overall coherence and logical flow are evident.
Running simulations, computing bounds and integrating psychological commentary indicate moderate creativity and initiative.  The approach is thoughtful but not exceptional in scope or originality.

The discussion of why the bankerâs percentage rises, how removing high boxes affects EV, and a critique of contestant behaviour shows thoughtful but not highly insightful analysis.  Arguments are sound yet remain at an intermediate depth.

Strengths and limitations are explicitly listed, and the student evaluates how sample size and incomplete round analysis affect conclusions.  Future implications are only lightly touched, so evaluation is âclearâ rather than âcomprehensive.â

Reflection appears in a dedicated section and intermittently throughout; the student comments on fairness, volatility, and psychological manipulation, demonstrating meaningfulâthough not deepâreflection.

Standard notation for expected value, permutations and linear regression is attempted, but inconsistently typeset (e.g., \Sigma\cdot P(x), misplaced dollar signs).  Variable names are sometimes omitted and sizeable numeric strings are hard to parse.  Correct notation appears often enough to meet âsome appropriate use,â but not with the consistency demanded for higher bands.

The student frames the study around a personal observation (sessions with trainer) and selects which variables to analyse, showing a clear personal approach.  However the methodology is conventional for this topic, so the work is not highly innovative.

Most content is relevant to modelling the bankerâs offer, yet lengthy anecdotal paragraphs, repeated EV formula lines, and over-long quotations on psychology add unnecessary bulk.  Had these been trimmed, the paper would be concise; as presented, it is only âmostly relevant.â

Expected value, permutations, linear regression and percentage bounds are all directly relevant to the question and mostly used appropriately.  The work goes beyond trivial arithmetic but does not reach the breadth or depth implied by the higher bands.

Designing and running 25-game simulations, deriving personal regression models, and critiquing banker strategy reflect genuine independent thinking, yet the mathematics remains fairly standard and errors limit depth.  This aligns with âsignificant evidenceâ rather than outstanding.

A contents page, labelled headings and sub-headings, and a closing evaluation demonstrate good organisation.  Diagrams and tables are generally placed near the text that refers to them.  Some sections (e.g., Simulation Round 2) feel abruptly cut and the bibliography formatting is uneven, but the overall structure is definitely clear and functional rather than merely âbasic.â

All mathematics sits comfortably within the SL syllabusâprobability with and without replacement, basic statistics, linear modelling.  Nothing is below the course level, yet there is little that stretches beyond it, matching âcommensurate with course level.â

annotations.comment: The aim statement is broad and could be more focused. Consider refining this into a specific research question that clearly defines the variables and scope of your exploration.
The anecdotal paragraphs on personal training are engaging but overly long. Trim or integrate more directly to maintain conciseness and relevance to the mathematical investigation.
Table 1 lists only one pair of box values. To improve clarity, include the full list of red and blue box values or adjust the caption to explain that the table is illustrative.
The rules text states there are 13 red and 13 blue boxes, but the show uses 26 unique values. Verify this with the game format to avoid misapplication of probability.
The introduction to your research combines simulation, regression, and psychology without stating a precise question. Formulate a clear, testable hypothesis to guide your analysis.
You introduce fear and control factors but do not quantitatively link these psychological models to your simulation data. Consider integrating metrics or case studies to bridge theory and results.
In Equation 4, the notation $$\Sigma \cdot P(x)$$ lacks an index. Use standard notation: $$E(X)=\sum_{i=1}^n x_iP(x_i)$$ to improve mathematical precision.
Listing each term of the EV individually in Equation 5 is verbose. Condense the calculation using a summation symbol, e.g., $$E(X)=\sum_{i=1}^{26}\frac{x_i}{26}$$.
The final EV result is stated as $131477 without formatting or units. Include the dollar sign, delimiters (e.g., commas), and indicate rounding precision.
The caption âTable 2: Deal or No Deal Gameâ does not explain the data. Rename it to reflect content, e.g., âTable 2: Round-by-Round EV vs. Banker Offer.â
The header '`ase' in the table on page 9 appears to be an OCR error or placeholder. Correct all table headers to meaningful labels.
The scatterplot lacks axis labels. Clearly label the x-axis (Round Number) and y-axis (% of EV) with units to improve interpretation.
Employing permutation formula $$P(n,r)$$ to compute probability conflates arrangements with probability. Use combinations or direct probability formula to find chances of selecting the $400{,}000.
The text introduces permutations without explaining why order matters here. Clarify the logic behind selecting permutations versus combinations for probability.
Table 3 (Bankerâs Rules) presents rules without citing evidence. Provide source attribution or derive rules directly from your data to strengthen validity.
The simulation text notes 25 games but doesnât justify this choice. Discuss why 25 runs are sufficient and any impact of sample size on your conclusions.
Your discussion of the regression model omits key statistics such as RÂ² and p-values. Include these to demonstrate the strength and significance of your fit.
The lower-bound EV calculation uses a denominator of 20 cases, but earlier EV was over 26. Ensure consistency in your base when removing cases.
When calculating the upper bound, clarify that you are applying the same regression line rather than refitting. This will avoid misunderstanding of methodology.
Round 2 table repeats headings from Round 1. Update caption numbering or table titles to avoid duplication and maintain clear organization.
In your Round 2 regression, you denote the independent variable as âXâ here and âxâ elsewhere. Choose one symbol and apply it consistently.
The lower-bound E(X)=561.07 for Round 2 seems unusually low given the data. Re-examine your EV calculation for the remaining cases.
Final Results table shows inconsistent decimal places (e.g., 56.061 vs 20.46). Standardize formatting of numerical values for consistency.
Your reflection on psychological unfairness is thoughtful but remains general. Anchor your insights with specific data points to deepen critical reflection.
In Evaluation strength 3 you state Pearson correlation x<0.9 indicates high significance. Typically r>0.9 denotes very strong correlation; adjust interpretation accordingly.
Bibliography entries use mixed formats and inconsistent punctuation. Adopt a single citation style (e.g., APA or MLA) and apply it uniformly.
